one_line_takeaway: The first NeurIPS unlearning competition and its DP-inspired, attack-based forgetting-quality metric show that top new algorithms genuinely outperform prior state-of-the-art, while exposing that rigorous unlearning evaluation is costly and that popular proxies like the accuracy gap are unreliable.
key_finding: Several top competition entries surpass prior state-of-the-art unlearning algorithms under both F-score and the utility-adjusted final score, indicating genuine progress. Crucially, the ranking of leading methods is stable across the cheaper evaluation setups (all except 'Reuse-N-1', the cheapest one used during the actual competition, which underestimates the most deterministic method, Fanchuan), pointing to viable compute savings. The commonly-used 'accuracy gap' proxy correlates poorly with F and is judged a bad proxy for forgetting quality per Definition 2.1. The best-forgetting method (Sebastian) pays the largest utility penalty because it prunes 99% of weights. Most winning algorithms share an 'erase' phase followed by a 'repair' phase.
core_contribution: A report on the first NeurIPS'23 machine-unlearning competition that makes two intertwined contributions: (1) an operational, differential-privacy-inspired (epsilon, delta)-unlearning definition and a practical evaluation framework that measures 'forgetting quality' F by mounting membership-inference-style attacks to distinguish the output distributions of an unlearned model (theta_u) from a retrained-from-scratch model (theta_r) on each forget-set example, then combines F with a utility adjustment into a final score; and (2) an extensive empirical analysis of the competition's top solutions (ranked 1-8, minus 5th place which was not reproducible) against five state-of-the-art unlearning baselines under this framework. The paper argues that evaluating unlearning is itself an open research problem and that its framework is a step toward standardized, principled measurement.
threat_model: The framework operationalizes forgetting as resistance to an adversary. Following the DP hypothesis-testing view (Kairouz et al.), a per-example attacker observes a scalar, logit-scaled 'confidence' output of a model on a forget-set example s and tries to decide whether that model was produced by unlearning U(A(D),S,D) or by retraining A(D\S). Success of unlearning = failure of the strongest attacker (high FPR+FNR). The attacker family comprises single-threshold and double-threshold rules swept over many thresholds; the per-example epsilon is taken from the strongest attack (Algorithm 2). This is closely related to membership inference attacks. The motivating real-world scenario is a GDPR-style deletion request: users whose facial images trained an age predictor ask that their data's influence be removed without full retraining.
limitations: By the authors' own account (Section A.1): the principled evaluation is compute-intensive, which is self-defeating if evaluating an approximate method costs more than retraining. The study is limited to two unlearning problems (CASIA-SURF and FEMNIST), a single architecture, and a single training algorithm. Competition methods were iterated specifically to improve this metric, whereas SOTA baselines were designed for other applications/metrics, so the comparison is somewhat favorable to competition entries. The generalizability test on FEMNIST uses only direct-transfer or 'light' tuning (3 values x 3 hyperparameters), so poor FEMNIST results do not prove a method cannot do well if tuned extensively. Design choices in the attack families, the epsilon-aggregation (binning) scheme, and the utility-combination formula are acknowledged as heuristic and not claimed optimal. The (epsilon, delta) values are empirically estimated, not theoretically guaranteed (approximate unlearning).

datasets_used: CASIA-SURF (facial-image age-prediction task; primary competition/evaluation dataset)
FEMNIST (Federated Extended MNIST, modified to match CASIA-SURF size/properties; used only for the generalizability study Q5)
quantitative_results: Participation: 1,338 individuals from 72 countries; 1,121 teams; 1,923 submissions (competition hosted on Kaggle Sept 11-Nov 30, 2023)
Main-experiment evaluation configuration: setup 'Full', N=1024, E=20 (E=10 for the SOTA comparison in Figure 5)
Competition evaluation used the cheapest setup 'Reuse-N-1' with N=512, E=1
Runtime cutoff: unlearning limited to roughly 20% of retrain-from-scratch time
Bootstrapping default pool size K = 8N (K < E x N), giving substantial savings over setup 'Full'
Finetune baseline has forget-set accuracy 93.6% vs retrain-from-scratch 85.6%, and scores worst on both F and accuracy-gap metrics (it overestimates retrain's forget accuracy)
Best-forgetting method 'Sebastian' prunes 99% of weights and is the most utility-penalized
Methods analyzed: leaderboard ranks 1-8 excluding 5th place (not reproducible)
baselines_compared: Finetune (finetune original model on retain set, relying on catastrophic forgetting)
NegGrad+ (gradient ascent on forget / descent on retain, cross-entropy; from SCRUB paper)
SCRUB (gradient ascent/descent with distillation from original model)
Random Label (assign random labels to forget set + finetune, plus added repair phase)
SalUn (Random-Label variant updating only salient weights by gradient magnitude)
